content.text: The Reward Model

The reward model (RM) is the learnable proxy for human judgment at the heart of RLHF. Its job is to score any (prompt, response) pair with a single scalar r(x, y) that predicts how much a human would prefer that response. The PPO loop then optimizes the language model policy to maximize this scalar, subject to a KL constraint that keeps the policy close to a reference baseline.

Getting the reward model right matters more than almost any other RLHF decision. A misspecified reward model produces a misaligned policy — the policy will find and exploit the reward model's blind spots, a phenomenon called reward hacking.

attrs.alt: Reward model architecture: tokenized (prompt+response) → LM backbone → last hidden state (EOS position) → Linear scalar head → r(x,y). Training: Bradley-Terry loss on chosen/rejected pairs.

content.text: Architecture
A reward model is built by taking a pre-trained language model and replacing its language-modeling head with a linear layer that maps the final hidden state to a scalar:

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from transformers import AutoModel, AutoTokenizer, AutoConfig

class RewardModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, base_model_id: str, freeze_backbone: bool = False):
        super().__init__()
        self.tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(base_model_id)
        self.backbone  = AutoModel.from_pretrained(base_model_id)
        hidden_size    = self.backbone.config.hidden_size

        # Replace LM head with scalar regression head
        self.value_head = nn.Linear(hidden_size, 1, bias=False)
        nn.init.zeros_(self.value_head.weight)   # start neutral

        if freeze_backbone:
            for p in self.backbone.parameters():
                p.requires_grad_(False)

    def forward(
        self,
        input_ids: torch.Tensor,        # (B, T)
        attention_mask: torch.Tensor,   # (B, T)
    ) -> torch.Tensor:                  # (B,) scalar rewards
        outputs = self.backbone(
            input_ids=input_ids,
            attention_mask=attention_mask,
        )
        # Use the last non-padding token's hidden state
        # For left-padded sequences, last token = position of last 1 in attention_mask
        seq_lengths = attention_mask.sum(dim=1) - 1          # (B,)
        last_hidden  = outputs.last_hidden_state              # (B, T, d)
        # Gather the last valid position for each sample
        gathered = last_hidden[
            torch.arange(last_hidden.size(0), device=last_hidden.device),
            seq_lengths
        ]   # (B, d)
        return self.value_head(gathered).squeeze(-1)          # (B,)

The EOS (end-of-sequence) token position is used as the reward read-out point for decoder-only models. Because the transformer's self-attention is causal, the EOS hidden state aggregates information from all preceding tokens in the sequence — prompt plus response.
Training Objective
The reward model is trained on a dataset of (prompt x, preferred response y_w, rejected response y_l) triples using the Bradley-Terry loss:
The gradient of this loss with respect to the reward difference is:

When the reward model is already confident (r_w ≫ r_l, P close to 1), the gradient is near zero — the pair contributes little to training. When the model is uncertain (r_w ≈ r_l), the gradient is large. This is the desirable property: the model learns most from ambiguous pairs.

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch.optim import AdamW

def bt_loss(r_w: torch.Tensor, r_l: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Bradley-Terry loss. r_w and r_l are scalar reward tensors of shape (B,)."""
    return -F.logsigmoid(r_w - r_l).mean()

# Training step
def train_step(
    model: RewardModel,
    optimizer: AdamW,
    chosen_ids: torch.Tensor,
    chosen_mask: torch.Tensor,
    rejected_ids: torch.Tensor,
    rejected_mask: torch.Tensor,
) -> float:
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    r_w = model(chosen_ids, chosen_mask)
    r_l = model(rejected_ids, rejected_mask)
    loss = bt_loss(r_w, r_l)
    loss.backward()
    torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0)
    optimizer.step()
    return loss.item()
Tokenization Strategy
The reward model sees the full (prompt + response) sequence as a single input. How this is formatted matters:
Use the same chat template as the SFT model:
if the SFT model was trained on Llama-3 chat format, the reward model must see the same format so that token positions have the same meaning.
Truncate from the left, not the right:

for long conversations, truncating from the left discards the beginning of the prompt. Since the response is what the reward model is scoring, right-side truncation (into the response) is worse. Prefer truncating the prompt middle or earliest turns.
Consistent EOS handling:
ensure the EOS token is always present at the end of every sequence — the reward is read from that position.

def tokenize_preference_pair(
    tokenizer,
    prompt: str,
    chosen: str,
    rejected: str,
    max_length: int = 2048,
) -> dict:
    """
    Tokenize a (prompt, chosen, rejected) preference triple using the model's chat template.
    Truncates from the left (prompt side) if necessary.
    """
    def apply_template(response: str) -> str:
        messages = [
            {"role": "user",      "content": prompt},
            {"role": "assistant", "content": response},
        ]
        return tokenizer.apply_chat_template(
            messages, tokenize=False, add_generation_prompt=False
        )

    chosen_text   = apply_template(chosen)
    rejected_text = apply_template(rejected)

    def encode(text: str) -> dict:
        ids = tokenizer(
            text,
            max_length=max_length,
            truncation=True,
            padding="max_length",
            return_tensors="pt",
        )
        return ids

    return {
        "chosen_input_ids":      encode(chosen_text)["input_ids"],
        "chosen_attention_mask": encode(chosen_text)["attention_mask"],
        "rejected_input_ids":      encode(rejected_text)["input_ids"],
        "rejected_attention_mask": encode(rejected_text)["attention_mask"],
    }
Hyperparameters and Training Setup
Hyperparameter
Typical value
Notes
Base model size
Same as policy or smaller
7B RM for 7B policy is common; 1B RM can be sufficient for narrow tasks
Learning rate
Lower than SFT; reward models overfit faster
Batch size
64–256 pairs
Larger batches → smoother BT gradient estimates
Epochs
Use early stopping on held-out preference accuracy
Max sequence length
1024–2048 tokens
Truncate prompt if needed; always keep full response
Weight initialization
From SFT checkpoint
Not the base LM — SFT weights give a better starting point for scoring
Gradient clipping
max_norm=1.0
Stabilizes training on outlier preference pairs

Initialize the reward model from the SFT checkpoint, not the base pre-trained model. The SFT model already understands the response format and the task domain, giving the reward model a head start in distinguishing response quality.
Evaluation Metrics
Preference accuracy
The primary metric is how often the reward model assigns a higher score to the preferred response:
A freshly initialized reward model guesses at 50%. A useful reward model reaches 70–85% on a held-out validation set. Higher than 90% on the training set but 70% on validation indicates overfitting.
Reward score distribution
Inspect the distribution of reward scores on a held-out set. A healthy reward model shows:
A clear gap between mean(r_w) and mean(r_l) — typically 1–3 units
Reasonable spread within each group (not all rewards collapsed to a point)
No catastrophic outliers (scores of ±20 suggest numerical instability)

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

def plot_reward_distributions(r_w_scores: list[float], r_l_scores: list[float]):
    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4))
    plt.hist(r_w_scores,  bins=50, alpha=0.6, label="Chosen (r_w)",   color="green")
    plt.hist(r_l_scores, bins=50, alpha=0.6, label="Rejected (r_l)", color="red")
    plt.axvline(np.mean(r_w_scores),  color="darkgreen", linestyle="--",
                label=f"mean r_w = {np.mean(r_w_scores):.2f}")
    plt.axvline(np.mean(r_l_scores), color="darkred",   linestyle="--",
                label=f"mean r_l = {np.mean(r_l_scores):.2f}")
    plt.xlabel("Reward score")
    plt.ylabel("Count")
    plt.title("Reward model output distribution")
    plt.legend()
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig("reward_dist.png", dpi=120)
Using TRL's RewardTrainer
Hugging Face TRL provides RewardTrainer, which wraps the BT loss, evaluation, and tokenization into a standard Trainer interface:

from trl import RewardConfig, RewardTrainer
from transformers import AutoModelForSequenceClassification, AutoTokenizer
from datasets import load_dataset

model_id = "meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct"

# AutoModelForSequenceClassification with num_labels=1 gives the scalar head
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
model = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(
    model_id,
    num_labels=1,
    torch_dtype="bfloat16",
)
tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token
model.config.pad_token_id = tokenizer.eos_token_id

# Load a preference dataset (must have 'chosen' and 'rejected' columns)
dataset = load_dataset("Anthropic/hh-rlhf", split="train[:10%]")

training_args = RewardConfig(
    output_dir="./rm-output",
    per_device_train_batch_size=4,
    gradient_accumulation_steps=8,   # effective batch = 32
    learning_rate=2e-5,
    num_train_epochs=1,
    max_length=1024,
    bf16=True,
    logging_steps=50,
    eval_strategy="steps",
    eval_steps=200,
    save_strategy="steps",
    save_steps=500,
    load_best_model_at_end=True,
    metric_for_best_model="eval_loss",
)

trainer = RewardTrainer(
    model=model,
    args=training_args,
    tokenizer=tokenizer,
    train_dataset=dataset,
)
trainer.train()
trainer.save_model("./rm-final")
Reward Model Failure Modes
Failure mode
Symptom
Mitigation
Verbosity bias
Longer responses get higher scores regardless of quality
Include gold pairs where shorter is correct; add length penalty during PPO
Sycophancy
Responses that flatter the user score higher
Include prompts where flattering answers are factually wrong
Format overfitting
Model learns to reward markdown formatting, bullet points
Include plain-text preferred responses in training data
Reward collapse
All responses converge to the same reward score
Check LR; reduce epochs; verify gradient flow to value_head
Out-of-distribution brittleness
Low accuracy on new task types not in training
Diversify prompt distribution; train on held-out domains
Reward Model vs Implicit Reward in DPO
DPO (Direct Preference Optimization) eliminates the explicit reward model by showing that the optimal policy under the KL-constrained RLHF objective induces an implicit reward:

In practice this means that in DPO, the policy model itself acts as the reward model — its log-probability ratio against the reference is the reward signal. The explicit reward model in PPO-RLHF serves the same mathematical role, but as a separately trained module. The trade-off:
PPO-RLHF (explicit RM)
DPO (implicit RM)
RM module
Separate model trained first
None; policy plays both roles
Online exploration
Yes — policy generates new rollouts
No — trains on fixed offline pairs
Distribution mismatch
Mitigated by online sampling
Risk if pairs are off-policy
Engineering complexity
High (4 models: policy, ref, RM, critic)
Low (2 models: policy, ref)
Reward hacking risk
High without careful KL tuning
Lower; no separate proxy to hack
Summary

A reward model is a pre-trained LM with its language-modeling head replaced by a linear layer that outputs a scalar. It is initialized from the SFT checkpoint and trained on preference pairs with the Bradley-Terry loss, which maximizes the log-sigmoid of the margin r_w − r_l. Evaluation focuses on held-out preference accuracy (target 70–85%) and reward score distributions. Common failure modes — verbosity bias, sycophancy, format overfitting — must be explicitly addressed through training data design. TRL's RewardTrainer provides a production-ready implementation. In DPO, the explicit reward model is replaced by the implicit reward encoded in the policy's log-probability ratio against the reference model.
